result:
The Museum of Unfinished Things

1.  The lobby is always drafty.  
A volunteer in a moth-eaten blazer stands by a portable heater, repeating the same sentence to every visitor: “You may touch anything you want, but nothing will touch you back.” Most people laugh, a few nod solemnly, one or two turn around and leave. Their coats retain the cold longer than physics says they should; outside is May, inside is the kind of March that makes bones argue with skin. Someone has left a half-empty coffee cup on the reception desk; the cup is part of the collection, labelled “Anonymous, 2012, ceramic and regret.” Nobody knows whether the label was written by the artist or by the curator. This is true of almost every label in the building.

2.  The building used to be a riverside grain exchange, then a propagandist printing house, then a community college for necromancy (unaccredited), then a squat, then a fire, then a hole, then a tax-deductible donation to the city. The city sealed the windows with plywood painted the color of apology. Grass grew on the roof; pigeons nested in the eaves; teenagers broke in and spray-painted “WE WERE HERE” in letters so large they could only be read from the opposite bank of the river, where the sentence looked like a horizon. Eventually a philanthropist—whose fortune came from inventing the little plastic tables that keep pizza boxes from collapsing—decided the city needed “a place where incompleteness could be honored without shame.” He paid to gut the interior, install motion sensors, and hire a curator whose previous job was archiving the dreams of Austrian civil servants. The curator’s first act was to remove the pizza-table fortune from the endowment, arguing that completion and sustenance were metaphors in conflict. The philanthropist died mid-sentence during the opening press conference; his last word was “crust.” The museum sold T-shirts printed with that word in Garamond. They sold out. The shirts came back in stock years later, minus the period. They never sold out again.

3.  The top floor is devoted to “Works Abandoned at 90%.” Here hang canvases with one corner blank, symphonies missing the final cadence, novels whose last chapters consist solely of the repeated phrase “INSERT CLIMAX HERE.” Visitors often experience vertigo: how can you tell 90% from 85 or 95? A graduate student once attempted to derive a formula using fractal dimensionality and the Gini coefficient of brushstroke distribution; she disappeared after presenting her findings at a conference in Dubrovnik. Her poster is now part of the exhibit, pinned between a jigsaw puzzle with one piece missing and a 3-D printer still extruding the top millimeter of Michelangelo’s David. The printer’s nozzle moves back and forth in the air like a sewing machine that has run out of fabric. A guard turns it off every evening; every morning it hums back to life, extruding nothing, calibrated to the absence of stone.

4.  The mezzanine is darker. It houses “Projects Whose Authors Forgot the Premise.” Visitors must sign a waiver acknowledging that memory loss is contagious. The air smells of wet notebook. Here you will find open cans of film labeled only “Reel 3,” floppy disks encrusted with barnacles, a python script that sorts a list into alphabetical order then immediately randomizes it again, over and over, as if rehearsing both bureaucracy and chaos. One vitrine contains a stack of postcards addressed to “Future Me,” all beginning “By the time you read this you will have…” The postmarks span thirty years; none were ever mailed. A postage stamp grows in the corner of each card like mold. The cards are arranged so the first lines form a spiral: “…you will have learned Hungarian… invented a new color… forgiven your father… built the boat… buried the dog… become someone else…” In the center of the spiral is a mirror so old it reflects you at ten years younger. Visitors either laugh or cover their eyes; the guard pretends not to notice.

5.  The staircase between mezzanine and ground floor is deliberately unfinished: missing every seventh step. Descending becomes an act of faith, ascending a test of hamstrings. The curator claims this is the most honest space in the museum. Sprinters training for psychological Olympics come here at dawn to rehearse liminality. They leave sweat on the absences, which is collected in small vials and sold in the gift shop as “Eau de Presque.” One sprinter, a woman who had run actual Olympics but lost the bronze by 0.003 seconds, tried to sponsor the missing steps, offering to cast them in bronze. The museum board vetoed the proposal, arguing that bronze would imply permanence. She left in tears; her tears are now catalogued under “Liquids That Almost Qualified,” shelf 4B, next to a bottle of champagne uncorked too early for Apollo 1.

6.  The ground floor is the largest wing: “Utopias Collapsed at the Blueprint Stage.” City plans printed on rice paper hang from ceiling tracks so they flutter when the doors open. One map shows a Paris redesigned by Charles Fourier in which the streets would be heated by perfumed lemon whales—an annotation in red ink objects that whales are mammals, not heaters. Another blueprint proposes an orbital library tethered to Earth by a staircase woven from diplomatic promises; the margins note that promises have tensile strength inferior to spider silk, superior to treaties. Visitors may unfold a laminated dining table whose legs are hollowed to grow potatoes; the chairs double as beehives. A curator’s note confesses the designer forgot human knees. Children love this room because they can crawl under the blueprints and invent their own cities with crayons provided in bins labeled “Insecurity” (red), “Hubris” (yellow), and “Improvisation” (invisible ink). The caretakers sweep up the crayons each night; by morning they have reproduced into new colors no manufacturer admits naming.

7.  The basement is soundproof. It must be. It houses “Apologies Never Sent.” The walls are lined with voicemails transcribed into light—sentences scroll across LED strips too fast to read except when you blink. Scientists have measured that the average visitor blinks in Morse code; collectively they spell “forgive me” every 4.7 minutes. The room contains 1,137 unaddressed envelopes, each holding a letter beginning “I was wrong about…” The envelopes age at different rates—some have turned yellow in months, others remain printer-fresh decades later. A robotic arm opens one envelope every hour, reads the letter aloud in a child’s voice, then burns the paper to ash which is immediately vacuumed into a glass orb. The orb hangs from the ceiling like a low moon. Over the years it has grown into a planet with its own weather: storms of sorry, gentle breezes of misunderstanding. Visitors regularly propose marriage under that orb; the curator marries them on the spot with vows taken from other unsent letters. No records are kept; the marriages last until the elevator reaches the lobby, sometimes longer in the gift shop.

8.  The gift shop is the most finished room in the museum, which is why staff apologize for it. You can buy postcards that arrive blank six months later, snow globes that contain the summer you were seven, pencils that can only draw missing objects. The bestseller is a pocket-sized notebook whose lines erase themselves overnight so you can reuse it forever. The manufacturer recommends writing your nightmares in it; the museum recommends writing your dreams. Both are gone by breakfast. The cashier is a former librarian who stamps each receipt with tomorrow’s date. When customers protest, she shrugs: “You haven’t finished the purchase yet.” The register totals always end in prime numbers; attempts to pay the exact amount result in the drawer opening to reveal a single copper coin minted the year of your first betrayal. People pocket the coin; it tarnishes into a photograph of the moment they decided not to say something crucial. The photograph is always blurry. It is the size of a pupil.

9.  Behind the gift shop is a door marked “Employees Only / Opción Continua.” The door is never locked because the lock was never invented. Staff step through into a courtyard open to the sky. The sky here is optional: sometimes stars, sometimes a fresco flaking into birds, sometimes the river that once powered the grain exchange flowing upside-down like a liquid aurora. In the center stands a kiosk that sells continuation. You may buy one sentence per week, payable in verifiable absence. Continuation comes in plain white cans; the label warns “Contents may settle after opening.” No one knows what happens if you finish a can. The intern who tried was promoted to curator and lost the ability to pronounce the word “the.” She now speaks in isometric verbs and is widely regarded as the most accomplished poet on staff. Her office is a broom closet whose walls are covered in letters she never sent herself; she claims they arrive through the faucet. When asked what continuation tastes like, she replies, “Like typing the last period then discovering the keyboard was imaginary.” She drinks it cold, from a chipped mug shaped like an hourglass missing its waist.

10. The staff itself is part of the collection. Their name tags list not names but the project they abandoned most recently: “Novel, pg 212,” “Marriage, year 6,” “Suicide note, third draft.” They wear the tags upside-down so visitors must tilt their heads, an ergonomic reminder. Every Friday they hold a potluck where each dish must include an ingredient that does not exist. Favorites are nostalgia for places you have never been, the sound of one hand clapping in a cathedral, and calorie-free forgiveness. Leftovers are composted into the museum’s foundation, which continues to grow sideways like a thank-you that swells until it becomes a burden. Architects have surveyed the structure annually for twenty years; each year they announce the building will collapse within twenty years. The prediction is always accurate and always ignored. The building responds by growing another hidden corridor, usually toward the river, as if rehearsing escape.

11. The river is the museum’s largest donor. It contributes driftwood shaped like question marks, bottles containing shopping lists from other centuries, single shoes still laced with the knot of hurry. Once it delivered a grand piano intact except for the absence of middle C; the instrument sits in a passageway, playing that absence whenever tide or memory rises. Children try to press the silent key; their fingers go straight through to tomorrow’s weather. A composer in residence has been transcribing the silent key for seven years. Her score is 4,000 pages of rests arranged in Fibonacci spirals. Orchestras refuse to perform it, claiming the silence is out of tune. She replies that the silence is exactly in tune with the museum, which is why nobody can hear it. She sleeps in a hammock strung between two time zones; her pillow is the piano’s missing hammer. Every morning she wakes to the sound of river applause, which sounds like footsteps on stairs that are not there.

12. There is, supposedly, a final room. A single wall label near the exit reads: “The Room of Completed Works—Temporarily Closed for Infinite Renovation.” Visitors sometimes report reaching it by accident: you mis-push on a fire door, or climb the mezzanine backwards, or fall through the orb of apologies into gravity that smells of typewriter ribbon. Allegedly the room is white, perfectly cubical, temperature fixed at the moment before crying becomes laughter. In its center stands a pedestal. On the pedestal is a glass cube. Inside the cube is a note the size of a postage stamp. On the note is written, in handwriting you almost recognize, the single sentence you most needed to read at the exact age when you stopped believing you were an artwork. Visitors who report seeing the room always add the same detail: the sentence ends with a comma, not a period. When they look back, the comma has become an open door the exact dimensions of their shadow. None has ever crossed the threshold; shadows cannot carry membership cards. Security footage shows only an empty corridor looping into itself like an inside-out Möbius strip. The curator insists the room is a myth invented by people who can’t tolerate the idea that incompleteness itself might be unfinished. She says this while wearing a lapel pin shaped like a comma. The pin is not for sale.

13. The museum issues no maps. Instead, visitors are handed a blank sheet of paper at the entrance and invited to fold it into whatever shape they believe they need: compass, paper plane, origami heart, crumpled ball to toss at a future that refuses to return the gesture. The fold lines are inked overnight into the building’s blueprints; by morning the corridors have shifted to accommodate new creases. Regular visitors learn to recognize their own folds in the architecture: here the hallway kinks where I made the wing, here the elevator stalls where I pressed too hard. The city’s fire marshal has condemned the practice as a hazard; the building responds by sprouting emergency exits that open onto the same lobby you left years ago, except the volunteer is now older, the heater finally warm, the coffee cup inexplicably fuller. People emerge blinking, uncertain whether they have aged or merely returned. The volunteer repeats the sentence: “You may touch anything you want, but nothing will touch you back.” This time nobody laughs.

14. Scholars debate whether the museum is a single living organism or a collective dream maintained by the insomnia of everyone who ever gave up. The river, the building, the staff, the visitors, the unfinished works—each asserts authorship. Admission is free on nights when no one remembers to close the gate; the gate has not existed since 1998. Closing time is announced by a single chime that sounds like a piano key you finally realize was never missing, only waiting for you to admit you can’t read music. People file out carrying plastic bags that did not belong to them when they entered. Inside each bag is a small object: a puzzle piece that fits no puzzle, a key whose teeth spell tomorrow, a comma pressed into clay. At home they place the object on a shelf and discover, days or decades later, that the shelf is now part of a corridor they once walked. They return to the museum to return the object, only to find the building has moved sideways by exactly the width of a sentence. The volunteer at the new entrance—sometimes the same face, sometimes the same face older—hands them a blank sheet of paper. The cycle continues, though cycle is the wrong word because it implies return, and return implies a center, and the museum refuses to be centered even on itself.

15. I went there once, or rather, I am going there. I left this account in the comments box, which is a shredder. The guard—her tag read “Letter I Never Mailed”—promised the strips would be composted into more corridor by Tuesday. I asked if I could bid on tomorrow’s sentence of continuation. She said the kiosk was fresh out, but I could have an overstock can of yesterday if I traded her the sound of my name spoken by someone who meant it. I told her I had already traded that years ago for an apology that arrived six months late and upside-down. She shrugged, recorded the transaction in a ledger bound in skin I preferred not to identify, and handed me a receipt that simply says “IOU the rest.” I folded it into a paper airplane small enough to fit inside a comma and threw it toward the missing step. It is still descending. When it lands, I expect to hear the soft percussion of arrival—unless arrival itself is the sound of one hand clapping in a cathedral, unless the cathedral is the museum, unless the museum is the pizza-box philanthropist’s last breath expanding into a lobby where a volunteer repeats, without accusation, that nothing will touch me back. I believe her now. I also believe that nothing is the most patient sculptor, caressing the air until the air agrees to become an object, an object agrees to become unfinished, and unfinished agrees to become a place we can walk into, carrying our blank sheets, ready to fold the next wing against gravity’s skin. The wing will not carry us. That is not its job. Its job is to keep open the parentheses we mistake for sky, the way a comma keeps open the sentence we mistake for life, the way the museum keeps open the city we mistake for real. Step inside. Bring your missing piece. Leave the map unfolded. The corridor is already waiting to learn the shape of your unfinished name.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
